hook: Romantic terraced gardens below the Alhambra where peacocks strut the paths
facts: Built atop the Nasrid Campo de Ahabul, where Christian captives were once held
Saint John of the Cross served as prior of a Carmelite convent here, 1582-1588
French financier Humbert Meersmans gave it its romantic gardens after 1891
Donated to the city in 1958; free to enter
w: Arab dungeons for Christian captives once stood on this hilltop; later a Carmelite convent where Saint John of the Cross was prior, then a French financier's pleasure grounds. Six terraced gardens, palm and arch and pond, drop below the Alhambra with peacocks underfoot. Free, and somehow nearly empty.

hook: Hushed 15th-c Nasrid palace of the last sultan's plotting mother
facts: Name means 'House of the Honest Lady'; home of Aixa, mother of Boabdil
From here she allied with the Abencerrajes against her husband Muley Hacén
After 1492 absorbed into the Santa Isabel la Real convent
Now open to visitors, often empty
legend: They say her ghost and a treasure linger in the walls of the abandoned palace, guarded against intruders.
w: From this hushed palace at the top of the Albaicín, Aixa, mother of the doomed Boabdil, schemed against her own husband the sultan. 'House of the Honest Lady.' Mirador windows frame the whole valley. Legend says her ghost and a buried treasure still guard the empty rooms. The kingdom fell anyway.

hook: The largest medieval cistern in the Albaicín, now a water museum
facts: Built in the 11th c by Zirid emir Badis to supply the old citadel
Holds 300 cubic metres across four vaulted underground chambers
Restored as a Water Interpretation Centre, opened 2009
Sits between the Ziri wall and Placeta del Cristo de las Azucenas
w: Granada ran on cisterns, and this one, sunk by a Zirid emir in the 11th century, ran the biggest of them: four vaulted chambers swallowing 300 cubic metres of water for the old citadel above. Cool, dark, basilica-shaped underground. Now a quiet little water museum behind the Ziri wall. The plumbing of an empire.

hook: Hilltop abbey over catacombs born of a 16th-c forgery and a treasure hunt
facts: Founded after 1595 lead-plaque discoveries about San Cecilio's martyrdom
The Santas Cuevas catacombs hold the 'Horno de San Cecilio' shrine
Plaques later judged forgeries by the Vatican
Crowns Monte Valparaíso at the end of the camino
legend: They say treasure-hunters digging in 1594 instead struck the relics and lead 'gospels' that made the hill holy.
w: Treasure-hunters digging Monte Valparaíso in 1594 hit ashes, bones and lead plaques 'proving' San Cecilio's martyrdom here. A whole abbey rose on the strength of it, atop catacombs and the saint's oven. Rome later ruled the plaques forgeries. Faith, fraud and a long climb, all on one hill.

hook: Eleven whitewashed cave-dwellings preserved as a museum of Roma life
facts: Set in the Barranco de los Negros ravine above the Darro
Eleven rock-cut houses furnished as homes and craft workshops
Shows basketry, smithing, ceramics and weaving trades
Cave-dwelling began here in the 16th century
w: Up the Barranco de los Negros, eleven whitewashed caves dug into the soft hill stand furnished as they were lived in: beds, looms, smith's tongs, the loft for the mule. For centuries Granada's Roma carved homes straight from the rock here. The Alhambra glows across the ravine. Cool inside, all year.

hook: Cushioned cave-like teahouse tucked off Granada's old tea street
facts: On Calle Calderería Nueva, the Albaicín's lane of teterías
Steps below the cathedral and San Nicolás climb
Mint tea, pastries and a back patio away from the crowd
Part of a Moroccan-style teahouse revival
w: Calderería Nueva sells itself hard, all hookah smoke and lanterns for the passing crowd, but duck the steps into As-Sirat and it goes quiet: low cushions, mint tea, a screened patio at the back. A small revival of Granada's Moorish teahouse habit, minus the elbows. Order the pastries and stay put.

hook: The highest, emptiest viewpoint over Granada, beside a lonely hermitage
facts: The Albaicín's highest viewpoint, with no obstructions
Neoclassical hermitage built 1815-1828 beside it
Sweeps the city, Vega, Alhambra and Sierra Nevada
A steep walk above the busy San Nicolás terrace
w: Everyone crams onto San Nicolás for the postcard; keep climbing past the city wall to the lone hermitage of San Miguel Alto and the whole valley opens with nobody in it. Albaicín, Alhambra, the Vega and the snow line, all at once. The highest view in Granada earns its hill.

hook: One of the oldest surviving Nasrid palace rooms, hidden in the Realejo
facts: Qubba hall dates from the reign of Muhammad II (1273-1302)
Once part of the 'Garden of the Great Wooden Wheel' estate
Excavated garden of myrtle and orange trees out front
Among the earliest Nasrid structures still standing
w: Older than most of the Alhambra's palaces, this Nasrid hall hides behind a plain Realejo wall: a 13th-century qubba with stucco and a tall tower, all that survives of a royal pleasure-garden named for its great wooden waterwheel. Excavators replanted the myrtle parterre. Half of Granada walks past, none go in.

hook: Baroque charterhouse with a sacristy of dizzying carved stone
facts: Founded 1506; built and decorated over some 300 years
Sacristy worked by Hurtado Izquierdo, 1727-1764
Sancta Sanctorum behind a Venetian glass gate
Called by some 'the most beautiful sacristy in the world'
w: Carthusians took a vow of silence and then spent three centuries shouting in stone. The sacristy here, all writhing brown-and-white marble and stucco by Hurtado Izquierdo, gets called the most beautiful in the world; the Sancta Sanctorum hides behind a Venetian glass gate. Restraint, abandoned entirely.

hook: Fortified 16th-c tower bristling with muskets, now a folk museum
facts: Built 1530-1540 for the Granada Venegas family
Name 'of the shots' from muskets jutting from its battlements
Part of the old Alfareros defensive wall
Gilded coffered ceiling in the Cuadra Dorada hall
w: Real muskets still poke from the battlements of this squat Renaissance tower, which is how it got its name, 'house of the shots.' Inside, the Cuadra Dorada drips with gilded coffering and the city's converso-noble history. A free folk museum that most visitors march straight past on their way uphill.

hook: The composer's preserved carmen, kept exactly as he left it
facts: Manuel de Falla lived here almost continuously, 1922-1939
On Calle Antequeruela Alta, just below the Alhambra woods
Composed much of his mature work in the house
Co-organised the 1922 cante jondo flamenco contest from here
w: The composer Manuel de Falla rented this little carmen under the Alhambra woods and barely left for seventeen years, writing his best work to a view of the Vega. Kept just as he had it, down to the piano and the garden. From here, with Lorca, he staged the deep-song contest that saved flamenco. His chair, still set.

hook: Square plague-shrine where the faithful ask three favours on Good Friday
facts: Cristo de los Favores carved 1640 at the residents' expense
Credited with sparing Realejo in the 1679-1682 plague
On Good Friday crowds make three petitions at 3 PM
Heart of the old Garnata al-Yahud Jewish quarter
legend: They say three wishes asked in silence before the Christ at three o'clock on Good Friday will be granted.
w: When plague gutted Granada in the 1680s, the Realejo stayed oddly untouched, and locals thanked the weathered Christ on the wall of this old Jewish-quarter square. They say three wishes asked in silence at three o'clock on Good Friday come true, so every year a hushed crowd tries. The rest of the year, tapas tables.

hook: The oldest, best-kept Arab baths in Spain, light falling through star vents
facts: Built in the 11th c under Zirid king Badis
Attributed to Jewish vizier Ibn Nagrela; 'Bath of the Walnut Tree'
Four rooms vaulted with star- and octagon-shaped skylights
Survived demolition, restored by Torres Balbás from 1918
w: The oldest Arab baths in Spain survived only because someone built a house on top and forgot them. Eleventh-century hammam vaults riddled with star-shaped vents drop coins of light onto bare brick and reused Roman columns. Cold room, warm room, hot room, no water now, just the quiet. On the Darro's loveliest stretch.

hook: The only intact Nasrid caravanserai left in Spain, hidden off a shopping lane
facts: Built in the 14th c as a merchants' inn and goods store
Later a coal yard, hence 'corral del carbón'
Then a comedy theatre and a tenement before rescue
Horseshoe gateway opens to a three-storey galleried patio
w: Push through a grand horseshoe arch off a humdrum shopping street and you are in the only Nasrid caravanserai left standing in Spain: a 14th-century traders' inn, three galleried storeys around a still courtyard. It served as a coal yard, a theatre, then slum housing before anyone noticed what it was. Free, and usually empty.

hook: Renaissance hospital of the Catholic Monarchs, once a madhouse
facts: Founded by royal charter in 1504, just after the conquest
Greek-cross plan with four patios round a central dome
From 1536 housed the 'Casa de Locos' for the insane
Now the University of Granada's rectorate
w: Ferdinand and Isabella raised this hospital the moment Granada fell, a Greek cross of four serene patios under a dome. It treated the pox, then from 1536 took in the city's mad after the old Muslim maristán shut. The university runs it now; students cross courtyards where the 'innocents' were once locked away.

hook: Gilded Renaissance church holding the tomb of the Gran Capitán
facts: Construction begun 1504; designed largely by Diego de Siloé
First church in the world consecrated to the Immaculate Conception
Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba, the Gran Capitán, lies under the altar
Two cloisters, one ringed with orange trees
w: Spain's great general, the Gran Capitán who reinvented warfare in Italy, lies under the altar of this blazing gold church, its walls a Siloé fever-dream of carved saints and battle trophies. The first church anywhere given to the Immaculate Conception. Step out to a silent orange-tree cloister. Power, gilded into prayer.

hook: Twin Morisco mansions with garden courtyards on the road to Sacromonte
facts: Two 16th-c Morisco houses on Nasrid foundations
Built by Lorenzo el Chapiz and Hernán López el Ferí
Home of the CSIC School of Arabic Studies since 1932
At the corner of Cuesta del Chapiz and Camino del Sacromonte
w: Two Morisco merchant houses, raised on Nasrid stone after the conquest, share a hillside of orchard and pool where the Albaicín tips into Sacromonte. Their builders kept Granada's Moorish craft alive in plaster and patios just as that world ended. Scholars of Arabic have worked here since 1932. The Alhambra watches from across the Darro.

hook: A tiny tucked-away terrace facing the Alhambra with almost no one on it
facts: Small mirador on Cuesta de San Agustín in the lower Albaicín
Frames the Alhambra and Generalife head-on
A quiet alternative to the packed San Nicolás terrace
Reached by stepped Albaicín lanes off the Darro
w: While the tour groups jam San Nicolás for the same photo, this little balcony on a stepped Albaicín lane keeps the Alhambra to itself: red towers and the Generalife's gardens straight ahead, a bench, a wall, silence. Half the people who pass don't even glance in. Bring a bottle and the dusk.

hook: Modest Albaicín square named for a Nasrid general lost in the war's last act
facts: Named for Aliatar, alcaide and father-in-law of Boabdil
Aliatar died at the 1483 battle of Lucena
A working neighbourhood plaza, not a monument
Near the old Aljibe de Trillo cistern and church of San Salvador
w: An ordinary Albaicín square, washing lines and a fountain, carrying the name of Aliatar, the fierce old general and Boabdil's father-in-law who rode out and died at Lucena in 1483, hastening the kingdom's fall. No plaque makes a fuss of it. Just a place where children play on the grave-name of a vanished war.

hook: Vast overgrown carmen on the red hill, hiding Alhambra dungeons and a Goya past
facts: Also called Carmen de Peñapartida, on the Mauror slope by the Hotel Alhambra Palace
Some 19,280 sq m of terraced garden, a quarter of the Alhambra's surrounds
Owners' family once held Goya's portrait of Isabel de los Cobos, now in London
Conceals some of the largest medieval dungeons of the Alhambra complex
w: Almost nobody gets into this rambling green carmen on the red hill, two football pitches of terraces and miradors clinging below the Alhambra. Its old owners once hung a Goya here; the lady's portrait now hangs in London. Underneath, some of the fortress's biggest dungeons. The Alhambra acquired it and opens it only rarely.
